risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A statistician generated a random allocation sequence of block size of four using a computer software program." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random method for sequence generation (computer-generated random sequence with blocked allocation), which is sufficient for low risk of bias for random sequence generation.

Quote: "A statistician generated a random allocation sequence of block size of four using a computer software program. The study coordinator enrolled participants. Educators assigned participants to interventions using sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed and equally weighted envelopes." Comment: This explicitly describes sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes used for assignment, which is an acceptable concealment mechanism that would prevent foreknowledge of the next allocation.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After assignment to interventions, only the study coordinator, who assessed the outcomes, was blinded." Comment: This explicitly indicates that participants and the educators/personnel delivering the intervention were not blinded after allocation. Because the study compared a decision aid plus education versus education alone, knowledge of group assignment could plausibly influence several outcomes assessed here (e.g. knowledge, decisional conflict, self-reported appropriate use of pharmacotherapy, and asthma control), so performance bias is at high risk overall.

Quote: "After assignment to interventions, only the study coordinator, who assessed the outcomes, was blinded." Comment: The outcome assessor was explicitly blinded. Most outcomes were self-administered questionnaires, face-to-face interviewer-administered assessments, and spirometry; because the study coordinator who assessed outcomes was blinded, knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence measurement of these outcomes.

Quote: "Forty-eight participants completed the study (education + DA group: n = 25; education group: n = 23). One participant of the education + DA group, who was hospitalized during the follow-up visit for an event unrelated to the study, was considered as lost to follow-up. All participants were included in data analysis." Comment: Attrition was very low (1/51, about 2%) and balanced enough to be unlikely to bias results; the single missing participant was lost for an unrelated hospitalization, and the analysis included all participants using intention-to-treat with mixed models that could handle missing follow-up data.
